voice_target: thoughtful operator who notices what actually makes owner acquisition hard, says it plainly, occasionally smirks at the absurdity, and does not try too hard to sound cool

tweets.text: A lot of owner hesitation has less to do with your pitch and more to do with how annoying switching managers sounds. Which is frustrating, because transition logistics are a much less fun thing to sell than upside.
If an owner says they need to think about it, I wouldn't assume you lost to another manager. A lot of the time they're picturing the hassle of switching and backing away from that.
One thing we keep seeing at Oratia: what sounds like fee pushback is often switching anxiety in a nicer outfit.

A question worth asking when an owner stalls: if you weren't working with your current manager at all, would this still feel like a hard decision? That usually tells you whether you're in a real comparison or just wrestling inertia.
If your follow-up email includes everything from the appointment, there's a decent chance you're helping the owner avoid the decision more professionally.
Feels like a lot of owner hesitation right now has less to do with commission and more to do with how annoying switching managers sounds. Are you seeing that too?
The status quo wins a shocking number of owner deals simply because it doesn't require an awkward email to the current manager. What creates more drag in your deals: fee pressure or switch friction?
I'm going deeper on this in the VRM Sales Library this week, because a surprising number of owner deals are really about switch friction in a nicer shirt.
tweets.notes: light connector tweet to bridge Twitter with the LinkedIn/newsletter series

tweets: A lot of owner deals don't die because another manager said something smarter.
They die because switching sounds like a hassle.
Awkward email to the current manager. Listing migration. Existing bookings. Spouse buy-in. The whole charming little parade.
Then the VRM responds with more upside. More revenue talk. More marketing. More tech.
If the hesitation is really about switching, the job is to make the move feel safer than staying put.
That means concrete transition language, a clear point of contact, and a visible safety net if things go sideways.
A lot of deals don't need a better pitch. They need a less annoying path to yes.
threads.notes: preferred thread shape: textured, specific, lightly dry, practical

style_notes: Prefer conversational observations over slogan-y punchlines
Use dry realism and shared-pain asides sparingly but deliberately
Favor operator recognition over generic catchiness
Let Oratia appear as context or pattern-recognition source, not as a pitch
Keep the account voice thoughtful, practical, and mildly self-aware
